evals.name: production-codebase-security-model
evals.prompt: I'm building a coding agent that will have access to our production source code and can execute commands. What security model should I use? I'm particularly worried about prompt injection via imported packages and accidental credential exposure.
evals.expected_output: A concrete security design covering: sandbox isolation (network egress controls, filesystem mounts), the lethal triad threat model (private data + untrusted content + external comms), semantic command parsing over keyword blacklists, principal loyalty definition, and trust-boundary enforcement below the application layer. Should address prompt injection via packages specifically and credential handling.
expectations: Addresses sandbox isolation with specific controls (network egress, filesystem mounts)
Covers the threat model combining private data access + untrusted content + external communication
Addresses prompt injection via imported packages specifically
Recommends keeping credentials out of the sandbox (never mounted)
Mentions semantic parsing over keyword blacklists for shell command safety
Addresses principal loyalty — who the agent serves
Recommends trust enforcement below the application layer (DB constraints, etc.)

evals.name: stuck-loop-error-recovery-design
evals.prompt: My coding agent keeps getting stuck — it tries the same fix over and over when something fails, and sometimes exhausts its budget without producing working code. How should I design the error handling and recovery system?
evals.expected_output: A structured failure recovery design covering: detection mechanisms (repeated-call fingerprinting, consecutive failure counters, idle watchdog), recovery escalation (silent retry -> degrade -> surface to user), circuit breakers and death-spiral defense, global caps (max turns, budget), and the principle that tool errors should feed back to the model rather than terminate the agent.
expectations: Identifies repeated-call fingerprinting as the mechanism to detect stuck loops
Describes recovery escalation: silent retry first, then degrade, then surface to user
Recommends circuit breakers with max consecutive failure limits
Addresses budget exhaustion with global caps (max turns, budget cap)
States that tool errors should become model input, not stop signals
Mentions death-spiral defense (disabling model-invoking side effects on error paths)

evals.name: minimal-toolset-grep-vs-semantic
evals.prompt: I'm starting a new coding agent project. What's the minimum set of tools I need to give it, and should I include semantic code search or is grep enough? The codebase is about 500k lines of Python.
evals.expected_output: The 7-tool minimal toolset (Code Interpreter, Bash Shell, Read/Write/Edit File, Search File Name, Search File Content), with reasoning for each. For 500k lines Python: recommendation on grep vs semantic search with concrete guidance — grep is sufficient for known identifiers, semantic search adds value for concept-level queries but is an optimization not a requirement. Should reference the search strategy tradeoffs.
expectations: Lists the 7-tool minimal toolset (Code Interpreter, Bash, Read, Write, Edit, Search File Name, Search File Content)
Explains why each tool earns its place (not just a list)
Addresses the grep vs semantic search question for a 500k line Python codebase
States that semantic search is an optimization, not a requirement
Explains when grep is sufficient (known identifiers, error messages, imports)
